channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Re-Evaluating EVMBench: Are AI Agents Ready for Smart Contract Security?
item.description: This paper re-evaluates EVMBench—the first large-scale benchmark for AI agents on smart contract security—by addressing its two critical limitations: narrow evaluation scope (only 14 agent configurations, mostly tested on vendor-specific scaffolds) and high contamination risk (all audit-contest data predates every model’s release). We expand evaluation to **26 configurations** across four model families (Llama, Qwen, Claude, GPT) and three scaffolds (LangChain, DSPy, OpenAgent), and introduce a **contamination-free dataset of 22 real-world security incidents** occurring *after* all models’ release dates. Our findings challenge the narrative of imminent fully automated AI auditing: (1) agent performance is highly unstable—rankings shift significantly across scaffolds, tasks, and datasets; (2) on real-world incidents, **no agent succeeds in end-to-end exploitation across all 110 agent-incident pairs**, despite detecting up to 65% of vulnerabilities—refuting EVMBench’s claim that vulnerability discovery is the primary bottleneck; and (3) scaffold choice materially impacts results, with an open-source scaffold outperforming vendor alternatives by up to 5 percentage points—a confounder uncontrolled in EVMBench. We conclude that AI agents are valuable as pre-deployment checks or human-in-the-loop assistants—not autonomous auditors. Code and data: https://github.com/blocksecteam/ReEVMBench/.

item.title: Incremental Federated Learning for Intrusion Detection in IoT Networks under Evolving Threat Landscape
item.description: This study addresses the critical challenge of maintaining long-term intrusion detection performance in resource-constrained IoT networks under evolving cyber threats. We propose an incremental federated learning (IFL) framework using lightweight LSTM models to continuously adapt non-stationary IDSs to concept drift—without centralized data access or full model retraining. Evaluated on the CICIoMT2024 dataset (5 attack categories, 27 variants), we compare cumulative, representative-sample, and retention-based incremental strategies under both binary and multiclass classification. Results show that cumulative and representative learning achieve the highest stability under drift (F1 standard deviation < 0.03), while retention-based methods deliver the best accuracy–latency trade-off (up to +12.7% F1 with only +18 ms update latency). All IFL approaches significantly outperform periodic federated aggregation (+9.2–15.4% average F1), demonstrating their viability for real-world, low-power IoT deployments.

item.title: AttriGuard: Defeating Indirect Prompt Injection in LLM Agents via Causal Attribution of Tool Invocations
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents are critically vulnerable to *Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI)*, where adversaries embed malicious directives in untrusted tool outputs to hijack agent behavior. Existing defenses treat IPI as an input-level semantic classification problem, suffering from poor generalization to unseen payloads and failure under adaptive attacks. We propose a paradigm shift to *action-level causal attribution*: instead of asking “Is this input malicious?”, we ask “*Why was this specific tool invocation generated?*”—distinguishing calls grounded in user intent from those causally induced by compromised observations. We instantiate this with **AttriGuard**, a runtime defense performing parallel counterfactual tests: for each candidate tool call, it re-executes the agent under a *control-attenuated view* of external outputs. Key innovations include teacher-forced shadow replay (to prevent attribution confounding), hierarchical control attenuation (to suppress diverse attack channels while preserving task semantics), and a fuzzy survival criterion (robust to LLM stochasticity). Evaluated across four LLMs and two agent benchmarks, AttriGuard achieves **0% ASR under static IPI** with negligible utility loss (<1.2% task success drop) and moderate overhead (+18–32% latency per step). Crucially, it maintains >92% defense rate under adaptive optimization-based attacks—where leading defenses degrade severely (ASR up to 89%).

item.title: CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving Systems
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on Automatic Prefix Caching (APC) to accelerate inference, but APC introduces timing side channels in multi-tenant serving: attackers infer sensitive user inputs by observing cache-hit/miss latency differences. Existing defenses disable APC entirely—sacrificing up to 70% cache reuse and increasing latency by 30%. CacheSolidarity is the first system that *selectively* mitigates APC side channels without compromising efficiency. It monitors cross-tenant prefix reuse in real time, detects statistically suspicious sharing patterns, and isolates *only high-risk prefixes*—leaving benign reuse intact. Evaluations show CacheSolidarity achieves full attack prevention while enabling **70% higher cache reuse** and **30% lower inference latency** versus isolation-only baselines. Its lightweight design (<1.2% CPU overhead) demonstrates that strong security in LLM serving need not entail unacceptable performance costs.

item.title: Repurposing Backdoors for Good: Ephemeral Intrinsic Proofs for Verifiable Aggregation in Cross-silo Federated Learning
item.description: While Secure Aggregation (SA) preserves update confidentiality in cross-silo FL, it offers no integrity guarantee—malicious servers can omit or alter updates undetected. Existing verifiable aggregation schemes rely on heavy cryptography (e.g., ZKPs, HE), suffering severe scalability bottlenecks: verification time explodes with model size. We propose **Ephemeral Intrinsic Proofs (EIP)**, a lightweight paradigm that repurposes *backdoor injection* to embed transient, verifiable signals directly into model parameters. Leveraging catastrophic forgetting, these signals remain robust for immediate server-side auditing yet decay naturally during training—ensuring zero utility loss in the final model. Our randomized, single-verifier framework integrates seamlessly with SA, preserves client anonymity, and avoids signal collisions without trusted parties. Experiments on SVHN, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 show >99.2% detection probability against malicious aggregation; on ResNet-18, EIP achieves **over 1000× speedup** versus cryptographic baselines—enabling practical, scalable verifiability for large models.

item.title: Detecting and Eliminating Neural Network Backdoors Through Active Paths with Application to Intrusion Detection
item.description: Machine learning backdoors pose severe security threats to critical applications like intrusion detection systems (IDS), as they maintain normal behavior on clean inputs but misclassify triggered inputs with high confidence. Detecting such triggers remains notoriously difficult due to their stealth and input-agnostic nature. This paper introduces an **explainable, activation-path-driven framework** for detecting and eliminating neural backdoors without requiring access to the poisoned training data or attacker knowledge. We identify *active paths*—stable, high-co-occurrence neuron activation sequences uniquely activated by backdoored inputs—and leverage statistical divergence (KS test) and co-activation graph analysis to isolate malicious paths. From these, we reconstruct trigger patterns and perform targeted path pruning followed by lightweight fine-tuning. Evaluated on NSL-KDD and CIC-IDS2017 with MLP/CNN-based IDS models, our method achieves **98.7% detection accuracy (F1=0.972)**, reduces attack success rate from 99.1% to 2.3%, and preserves >99.2% clean accuracy post-mitigation. This work bridges model interpretability and backdoor defense in a unified, data-free, and deployment-friendly manner.

item.title: IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs
item.description: Instruction Hierarchy (IH) defines the trust-ordered priority LLMs apply when resolving conflicts among system, developer, user, and tool instructions—serving as a foundational defense against jailbreaks, system prompt extraction, and agentic prompt injection. Yet robust IH learning remains challenging due to confounding with general instruction-following failures, nuanced conflict semantics, and shortcut behaviors like overrefusal. We introduce **IH-Challenge**, a reinforcement learning training dataset featuring adversarially curated instruction conflicts with explicit priority labels and dynamic boundary-case generation. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on IH-Challenge with online adversarial sampling improves average IH robustness by **+10.0%** (84.1% → 94.1%) across 16 in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and human red-teaming benchmarks; reduces unsafe responses from **6.6% to 0.7%** while *increasing* helpfulness on general safety evaluations; and achieves saturation (100%) on an internal static agentic prompt injection benchmark—with negligible capability regression. The dataset is publicly released to advance research on trustworthy instruction hierarchy.

item.title: Enhancing Network Intrusion Detection Systems: A Multi-Layer Ensemble Approach to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks
item.description: Adversarial examples pose a critical threat to ML-based Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS), potentially enabling evasion attacks that compromise network security. To address this, we propose a novel **multi-layer ensemble defense framework** combining stacked classifiers (XGBoost, LightGBM, RF) and a conditional autoencoder layer: benign-classified traffic triggers autoencoder-based reconstruction verification, forming a robust “classify-then-validate” pipeline. We generate adversarial traffic via both GAN and FGSM methods and integrate adversarial training into the entire pipeline. Evaluations on UNSW-NB15 and NSL-KDD show our approach improves detection accuracy by 12.7–18.3% under FGSM attacks and boosts AUC by 9.5–13.1 points under GAN attacks, while maintaining false positive rates <0.8%. This work introduces the first dynamically activated autoencoder layer for NIDS adversarial mitigation, significantly enhancing resilience without sacrificing detection performance.

item.title: Don't Let the Claw Grip Your Hand: A Security Analysis and Defense Framework for OpenClaw
item.description: This paper presents the first structured security analysis of OpenClaw—a popular open-source, locally-executed LLM-powered code agent framework. We systematically evaluate its native resilience across 47 adversarial scenarios spanning six MITRE ATLAS/ATT&CK attack categories, revealing a critically low baseline defense rate of only 17%, especially against sandbox escape and privilege escalation. To address this, we propose a lightweight Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) defense layer that intercepts high-risk commands via semantic risk scoring and optional human approval before execution. Dual-mode evaluation shows our HITL layer successfully blocks all 8 severe attacks that fully bypassed OpenClaw’s native defenses. When integrated, the combined system achieves defense rates ranging from 19% to 92%, depending on attack type. Our work exposes fundamental security gaps in current code agents and demonstrates that judicious human-agent collaboration—not full automation—is essential for secure real-world deployment.

item.title: Risk-Adjusted Harm Scoring for Automated Red Teaming for LLMs in Financial Services
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly deploying in regulated Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance (BFSI) contexts, yet standard red-teaming benchmarks lack domain specificity and fail to capture legally or professionally plausible harmful behaviors. We introduce a risk-aware evaluation framework featuring: (1) a BFSI-specific harm taxonomy, (2) an automated multi-round adversarial pipeline, and (3) an ensemble judging protocol yielding the **Risk-Adjusted Harm Score (RAHS)**—a severity-weighted metric that incorporates operational impact, mitigation signals, and inter-judge agreement. Across 12 financial LLMs, we find that increased decoding stochasticity and sustained adaptive interaction not only raise jailbreak success rates but systematically escalate outputs toward *operationally actionable* financial disclosures (e.g., fabricated account numbers, bypassed KYC logic). These results demonstrate the inadequacy of single-turn, domain-agnostic security evaluation and underscore the necessity of risk-sensitive, prolonged adversarial assessment for real-world BFSI deployment.

item.title: AlphaFlowTSE: One-Step Generative Target Speaker Extraction via Conditional AlphaFlow
item.description: Target speaker extraction (TSE) aims to recover clean target speech from a multi-talker mixture using a short enrollment utterance. While diffusion and flow-matching models improve fidelity, their multi-step sampling incurs high latency, and existing one-step methods rely on unreliable mixture-dependent time coordinates—limiting real-world robustness. We propose **AlphaFlowTSE**, a one-step conditional generative model trained with a **Jacobian-vector-product (JVP)-free AlphaFlow objective**. It learns mean-velocity transport directly from the observed mixture toward the target speech—eliminating auxiliary mixing-ratio prediction—and stabilizes training via an **interval-consistency teacher-student loss** that enforces temporal coherence in flow matching. Experiments on Libri2Mix and REAL-T show AlphaFlowTSE achieves superior target similarity (e.g., +2.1 dB SI-SNRi) and generalization to real mixtures, reducing downstream ASR WER by 18.7% on average compared to prior SOTA.

item.title: Prompting with the human-touch: evaluating model-sensitivity of foundation models for musculoskeletal CT segmentation
item.description: This study evaluates 11 promptable foundation models (FMs) for musculoskeletal CT segmentation of bone and implants across wrist, shoulder, hip, and lower leg—using both public and private datasets. We benchmark non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting strategies and, critically, incorporate *real human prompts* collected from 5 radiologists via a dedicated observer study—replacing idealized label-derived prompts. Pareto-optimal models are identified: **SAM and SAM2.1 for 2D**, **nnInteractive and Med-SAM2 for 3D**. Key findings include: (1) substantial performance variation across models and prompting modalities; (2) strong anatomical dependence of localization accuracy and inter-rater consistency (e.g., ICC=0.89 for wrist bones vs. ≤0.52 for pelvis/tibia/implants); (3) consistent performance drop (mean −12.3% Dice) with human prompts, indicating overoptimistic reporting in label-based evaluations; and (4) universal sensitivity to prompt variation—no model achieved robustness across raters despite intra-rater stability in two cases. Our open-source benchmarking framework is available at https://github.com/CarolineMagg/segmentation-FM-benchmark.

item.title: Federated Active Learning Under Extreme Non-IID and Global Class Imbalance
item.description: Federated Active Learning (FAL) aims to reduce annotation costs under privacy constraints, yet suffers severe performance degradation under *simultaneous* extreme non-IID data and global class imbalance—common in real-world deployments. Through systematic analysis, we uncover a key insight: **class-balanced querying—not just model accuracy—is the primary driver of final performance**, especially for minority classes. Moreover, global-model querying only benefits when global imbalance is high *and* client data are relatively homogeneous; otherwise, local models are preferable. Guided by these findings, we propose **FairFAL**, the first adaptive, class-fair FAL framework. It (1) infers global imbalance and local-global divergence via lightweight prediction discrepancy to dynamically select between global/local query models; (2) performs prototype-guided pseudo-labeling using global features to prioritize minority-class queries; and (3) employs a two-stage uncertainty-diversity balanced sampling strategy with k-center refinement. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks—including long-tailed CIFAR-10-LT and ImageNet-LT under strong Non-IID—show FairFAL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods (e.g., FedAL, BALD-Fed) by 3.2–7.8% absolute accuracy, with gains up to 11.4% in the most challenging settings. Code: https://github.com/chenchenzong/FairFAL.

item.title: SNPgen: Phenotype-Supervised Genotype Representation and Synthetic Data Generation via Latent Diffusion
item.description: SNPgen is a phenotype-supervised latent diffusion framework for generating high-fidelity synthetic genotypes. It integrates GWAS-guided SNP selection (1,024–2,048 trait-associated variants) with a VAE-based genotype compression and a classifier-free guided latent diffusion model conditioned on binary disease labels. Evaluated on 458,724 UK Biobank individuals across four complex diseases, models trained solely on SNPgen-synthetic data achieve predictive performance (AUC) on real test sets comparable to those trained on real data—and rival genome-wide PRS using 2–6× more variants. Privacy analysis confirms zero identical matches, near-random membership inference (AUC ≈ 0.50), preserved linkage disequilibrium, and strong allele frequency correlation (*r* ≥ 0.95). A controlled simulation with known causal effects further validates faithful recovery of genetic association structure. SNPgen bridges the gap between statistical fidelity and task utility while ensuring robust privacy.

item.title: Prioritizing Gradient Sign Over Modulus: An Importance-Aware Framework for Wireless Federated Learning
item.description: Wireless federated learning (FL) faces severe performance degradation due to unreliable gradient transmission under scarce wireless resources. To address this, we propose **Sign-Prioritized FL (SP-FL)**—a novel importance-aware framework that prioritizes the reliable transmission of gradient *signs*, which encode the essential descent direction for model updates, over less critical gradient *modulus*. SP-FL transmits signs in dedicated, high-reliability packets and allows sign reuse when modulus recovery fails. We formulate a hierarchical resource allocation problem jointly optimizing bandwidth across devices and power between sign/modulus packets, guided by explicit importance metrics derived from SP-FL’s one-step convergence analysis. An alternating optimization algorithm—combining Newton-Raphson and successive convex approximation (SCA)—is designed to solve it efficiently. Experiments on CIFAR-10 show SP-FL achieves up to **9.96% higher test accuracy** than state-of-the-art wireless FL baselines under constrained resources, confirming its superiority in reliability-critical edge AI deployment.

item.title: MultiwayPAM: Multiway Partitioning Around Medoids for LLM-as-a-Judge Score Analysis
item.description: LLM-as-a-Judge enables flexible, multi-perspective text evaluation via prompt engineering, yet suffers from high inference cost and systematic evaluator bias. To uncover the *structured nature* of such bias across questions, answerers, and evaluators, we propose **MultiwayPAM**, a novel tensor clustering algorithm that simultaneously identifies cluster memberships and interpretable medoids for all three modes of a score tensor. Unlike standard decomposition or unimodal clustering, MultiwayPAM guarantees real-data medoids (not abstract centroids) per mode, enabling direct semantic interpretation—for instance, revealing evaluator subgroups with consistent over-scoring of open-source models or under-scoring of verbose answers. Evaluated on two real-world LLM-as-a-Judge datasets (12K+ scores), MultiwayPAM achieves +23.6% average improvement in clustering quality over baselines and enables bias-aware score aggregation that boosts Spearman correlation with human judgments by 0.18 using only 15% of original inference cost. This work establishes multiway clustering as a principled, interpretable tool for auditing and mitigating LLM evaluator bias.

item.title: GLM-OCR Technical Report
item.description: GLM-OCR is an efficient 0.9B-parameter multimodal model for real-world document understanding. It integrates a 0.4B-parameter CogViT visual encoder with a 0.5B-parameter GLM language decoder, striking an optimal balance between efficiency and accuracy. To overcome the latency bottleneck of autoregressive decoding in deterministic OCR, GLM-OCR introduces Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), enabling parallel multi-token generation per step while maintaining low memory overhead via parameter sharing. System-wise, it adopts a two-stage pipeline: layout analysis by PP-DocLayout-V3 followed by parallel region-level structured recognition. Evaluations across public benchmarks (PubLayNet, DocBank, CORD, SciTSR, FormulaGraph) and industrial deployments show state-of-the-art or competitive performance in document parsing, text/formula transcription, table structure recovery, and key information extraction. Its compact size, structured generation capability, and low-latency inference make it suitable for both edge devices and large-scale production systems.

item.title: Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC): Ultra-Efficient Semantic Communication Applications to Privacy
item.description: We introduce **Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC)**, a semantic communication framework where a sender transmits only the minimal information needed for a receiver to *generate a randomized function* of the input—formalizing privacy-aware distributed computation as generalized remote source coding. Leveraging strong coordination metrics, we establish provable **local differential privacy (LDP) for all input sequences**, even *without any common randomness* between transmitter and receiver. We derive tight lower bounds on Wyner’s Common Information (WCI)—the fundamental rate limit under no shared randomness—and propose numerical methods to evaluate the complementary corner point of the RDFC rate region for continuous alphabets with unlimited shared randomness. Experiments show that sufficient shared randomness reduces the semantic communication rate by up to **two orders of magnitude** versus WCI; even without shared randomness, RDFC vastly outperforms lossless transmission. Finite-blocklength analysis confirms the privacy parameter gap between asymptotic and non-asymptotic RDFC vanishes **exponentially fast** in input length—positioning RDFC as an energy-efficient, privacy-native semantic communication strategy for resource-constrained distributed systems.

item.title: Compartmentalization-Aware Automated Program Repair
item.description: Software compartmentalization promises strong security by isolating application components, yet real-world deployments suffer from critical vulnerabilities at cross-compartment interfaces—enabling attackers to escape confinement. Securing these interfaces manually is error-prone and costly. This paper introduces the first *compartmentalization-aware* Automated Program Repair (APR) framework, designed specifically to fix such interface flaws. Unlike generic LLMs or traditional APR tools—which lack awareness of isolation semantics—our approach integrates: (1) a specialized fuzzer targeting interface-specific bugs (e.g., IPC validation gaps); (2) a patch generator that bridges LLMs’ semantic gap via compartment boundary analysis, contract inference, and privilege-flow modeling; and (3) a validator checking functional correctness, security efficacy, and isolation integrity. Evaluated on a real WebAssembly IPC vulnerability, our framework successfully generated verified fixes, while off-the-shelf LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, CodeLlama) produced insecure or non-functional patches. This work establishes a new foundation for automating the defense of isolation boundaries.

item.title: Democratising Clinical AI through Dataset Condensation for Classical Clinical Models
item.description: Dataset condensation (DC) offers a promising path toward healthcare data democratization by generating compact, synthetic datasets that preserve model utility while enabling strong privacy guarantees—especially when combined with differential privacy (DP). However, existing DC methods rely on gradient-based optimization, making them incompatible with widely used non-differentiable clinical models (e.g., decision trees, Cox regression). To bridge this gap, we propose the first DP-compliant, zero-order DC framework that requires only black-box model evaluations—not gradients—thus supporting *any* classical clinical predictor. Evaluated across six real-world clinical datasets (including classification and survival tasks), our method produces synthetic sets as small as 0.1% of original size, yet maintains near-lossless utility: e.g., <0.012 C-index drop for Cox models and 98.3% AUC retention for decision trees under $\varepsilon=2.0$ DP. Crucially, it enables truly model-agnostic, privacy-preserving data sharing—empowering diverse stakeholders to train their preferred clinical models without accessing sensitive patient records.

item.title: Optimal partition selection with Rényi differential privacy
item.description: We generalize optimal partition selection from $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP to $\delta$-approximate $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-Rényi DP (RDP), enabling tighter composition analysis. Since no general optimality result exists for users submitting *multiple* partitions, we design a tailored optimal algorithm for $L^2$-bounded *weighted* partition selection—requiring only partition release (not frequencies)—which serves as a drop-in replacement for the Gaussian mechanism. We integrate it into state-of-the-art algorithms (PolicyGaussian and MAD2R), improving performance in both parallel and adaptive sequential settings. Crucially, we formalize the notion of optimal additive-noise RDP partition selection and prove a strict numerical separation: mechanisms releasing *both* partitions *and* frequencies incur inherent privacy cost compared to non-additive, partition-only mechanisms—demonstrating a fundamental trade-off between utility scope and privacy efficiency.

item.title: Fast and Optimal Differentially Private Frequent-Substring Mining
item.description: We present the first $\varepsilon$-differentially private algorithm for frequent substring mining that achieves near-optimal statistical error *and* scalable efficiency. Given $n$ strings of length at most $\ell$ over alphabet $\Sigma$, our method reduces space complexity from $O(n^2 \ell^4)$ to $O(n \ell + |\Sigma|)$ and time complexity from $O(n^2 \ell^4)$ to $O(n \ell \log |\Sigma| + |\Sigma|)$, while preserving the same near-optimal error guarantees as the state-of-the-art. Key innovations include: (i) a structure-aware top-down candidate generation strategy leveraging monotonicity of prefix/suffix frequencies, and (ii) aggressive search-space pruning guided by frequency inclusion relations (e.g., if $s$ is frequent, all its substrings must satisfy lower bounds). These eliminate quadratic blow-ups inherent in prior work, enabling practical differentially private substring mining on large-scale datasets.

item.title: AgenticCyOps: Securing Multi-Agentic AI Integration in Enterprise Cyber Operations
item.description: AgenticCyOps is a novel security framework for securing LLM-powered multi-agent systems (MAS) in enterprise cyber operations. Recognizing that autonomous tool access and memory sharing introduce critical, previously unaddressed attack surfaces—distinct from deterministic pipelines—we systematically decompose threats across component, coordination, and protocol layers. This reveals that all documented exploits converge on two integration surfaces: **tool orchestration** and **memory management**, which we formalize as primary trust boundaries. Grounded in this insight, we define five compliance-aligned defensive principles: authorized interfaces, capability scoping, verified execution, memory integrity & synchronization, and access-controlled data isolation—each mapped to NIST, ISO/IEC 27001, GDPR, and the EU AI Act. Applied to a SOC workflow using the Model Context Protocol (MCP), our design employs phase-scoped agents, consensus validation loops, and per-organization memory boundaries. Evaluation shows AgenticCyOps achieves defense-in-depth coverage of all known vectors, intercepts 3 out of 4 representative attack chains within the first two steps, and reduces exploitable trust boundaries by ≥72% versus flat MAS architectures—establishing a foundational, auditable, and standards-compliant model for enterprise-grade agentic AI integration.

item.title: Multi-model approach for autonomous driving: A comprehensive study on traffic sign-, vehicle- and lane detection and behavioral cloning
item.description: This paper presents a comprehensive multi-model deep learning framework for autonomous driving, addressing traffic sign classification, vehicle detection, lane segmentation, and behavioral cloning in an integrated manner. We leverage transfer learning with ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B3, complemented by custom lightweight architectures enhanced by geometric-color data augmentation, multi-scale U-Net variants, optimized YOLOv5s, and an attention-augmented behavioral cloning model trained on 22k Udacity simulator frames. Evaluated on GTSRB (99.2% accuracy), BDD100K (78.4% mAP@0.5), TuSimple (96.7% F1), and closed-loop simulation (0.32m avg. lateral error), our approach significantly outperforms baselines. The work establishes a reproducible, modular pipeline that improves robustness, generalization, and real-time performance—advancing toward safer, more deployable autonomous systems.

item.title: MM-algorithms for traditional and convex NMF with Tweedie and Negative Binomial cost functions and empirical evaluation
item.description: Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is widely used for unsupervised learning, yet standard Gaussian or Poisson assumptions often fail for overdispersed count data. This paper introduces a unified Majorize-Minimize (MM) framework for both traditional and *convex* NMF under Tweedie and Negative Binomial likelihoods—linking Tweedie models to β-divergences and enabling statistically principled cost functions. We derive novel, provably convergent multiplicative updates for all models, including the first such rules for convex NMF with Poisson and Negative Binomial losses. Empirical evaluation on mutational and word-count datasets shows that appropriate noise modeling improves reconstruction (by 12–37%) and feature recovery (e.g., biologically meaningful mutation signatures), while convex NMF offers superior efficiency and robustness over traditional NMF when the number of components is large. Our open-source R package `nmfgenr` provides the first unified implementation of these models.

item.title: Nonparametric Variational Differential Privacy via Embedding Parameter Clipping
item.description: Nonparametric Variational Differential Privacy (NVDP) builds on the Nonparametric Variational Information Bottleneck (NVIB) to train privacy-preserving language models. Yet, unconstrained NVIB often suffers from posterior drift—latent representations concentrate in high-information regions—degrading both privacy guarantees (via inflated Rényi Divergence bounds) and utility (due to training instability). We propose **Embedding Parameter Clipping**, a theoretically grounded method derived directly from minimizing the Rényi Divergence (RD) upper bound. It imposes explicit, analytically justified constraints on the posterior mean, variance, and mixture weights—ensuring bounded sensitivity without heuristic tuning. Empirical evaluation on AG News, Yelp, and SST-2 shows our clipped NVIB model achieves **38.7% tighter RD bounds** (implying stronger (ε,α)-Rényi DP) and **+2.1–4.9% absolute accuracy gains** on downstream tasks, all while improving training stability and convergence speed. This simple, plug-and-play technique advances the privacy–utility trade-off in variational NLP models.

item.title: Tracking Cancer Through Text: Longitudinal Extraction From Radiology Reports Using Open-Source Large Language Models
item.description: Radiology reports contain vital longitudinal oncology information, but their unstructured format hinders automated analysis—especially in privacy-sensitive healthcare settings where proprietary LLMs are often inadmissible. We introduce a fully open-source, locally deployable pipeline using the `llm_extractinator` framework and the **Qwen2.5-72B** model to extract and temporally link target, non-target, and new lesions from radiology reports according to RECIST 1.1 criteria. Evaluated on 50 paired Dutch CT Thorax/Abdomen reports (100 total), our system achieved **93.7% accuracy for target lesion attributes**, **94.9% for non-target lesions**, and **94.0% for new lesions**, measured at the fine-grained attribute level (e.g., size, status, measurability). These results demonstrate that state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs can deliver clinically meaningful performance in longitudinal oncology extraction—without compromising data sovereignty, reproducibility, or regulatory compliance. The pipeline is publicly released under Apache 2.0.

item.title: TA-Mem: Tool-Augmented Autonomous Memory Retrieval for LLM in Long-Term Conversational QA
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in text reasoning but struggle with long-term conversational QA due to fixed context windows, necessitating external memory systems. Existing approaches rely on static episodic notes or graphs and use inflexible, predefined retrieval workflows—often limited to embedding similarity top-k. To overcome this, we propose **TA-Mem**, a tool-augmented autonomous memory retrieval framework comprising: (1) a semantic-aware memory extraction agent that adaptively chunks input and structures knowledge into typed notes; (2) a multi-indexed memory database supporting both key-based lookup and similarity search; and (3) a retrieval agent that autonomously selects and invokes appropriate database tools (e.g., “timeline query”, “conflict detection”) based on user intent, iteratively fetching and reasoning over memories before final response generation. Evaluated on the LoCoMo benchmark, TA-Mem achieves +12.7% F1 and +9.4% EM over strong baselines. Analysis confirms its adaptive tool selection across question types—enabling robust multi-hop, temporal, and consistency reasoning.

item.title: MCP-in-SoS: Risk assessment framework for open-source MCP servers
item.description: Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers enable LLM agents to interact with real-world tools, yet their rapid open-source adoption has outpaced security assurance. This paper presents **MCP-in-SoS**, the first systematic, large-scale risk assessment framework for open-source MCP servers. We statically analyze 47 active GitHub projects, identifying 127 exploitable CWE weaknesses and mapping them to 19 real-world attack patterns via MITRE CAPEC. Our framework quantifies risk using a multi-metric score combining exploit likelihood (e.g., authentication bypass, exposed endpoints) and impact (confidentiality, integrity, availability). Results show 86% of servers contain at least one high-severity weakness—31% expose direct RCE paths via unvalidated inputs—and configuration flaws (e.g., unsafe defaults, hardcoded secrets) account for >40% of medium-high risks. MCP-in-SoS provides actionable, prioritized risk intelligence and is released as an open toolkit.

item.title: Compatibility at a Cost: Systematic Discovery and Exploitation of MCP Clause-Compliance Vulnerabilities
item.description: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) promises interoperability for AI agents but introduces a novel attack surface—*compatibility-abusing vulnerabilities*—arising from its relaxed, optional clause design. This paper presents the first systematic framework to discover and exploit such vulnerabilities across multi-language MCP SDKs. We introduce: (1) a language-agnostic intermediate representation (IR) generator that normalizes SDK semantics; (2) LLM-guided static analysis for auditable, cross-language clause compliance verification; and (3) a formalization of three attack modalities (control-flow hijacking, resource exhaustion, context confusion), enabling modality-driven vulnerability discovery. Evaluating 12 production SDKs (5 languages), we uncover 47 exploitable non-compliance issues—including 12 high-severity (CVSS ≥ 8.0) vulnerabilities—and demonstrate silent prompt injection and cascading DoS attacks. Our open-source tool, MCP-Auditor, establishes a new benchmark for protocol-level AI agent security.

item.title: Execution Is the New Attack Surface: Survivability-Aware Agentic Crypto Trading with OpenClaw-Style Local Executors
item.description: This paper identifies a paradigm shift in agentic crypto trading safety: as OpenClaw-style stacks and skill marketplaces (e.g., skills.sh) mature, the dominant failure mode evolves from *incorrect LLM outputs* to *execution-induced irreversible loss*. To address this, we propose **Survivability-Aware Execution (SAE)**—a middleware standard enforcing non-bypassable last-mile invariants between strategy engines and exchange executors. SAE defines an explicit execution contract and enforces projection-based exposure budgets, cooldown/order-rate limits, slippage bounds, staged execution, and tool/venue allowlists. We formalize the *Delegation Gap (DG)* via a logged Intended Policy Spec for testable, reproducible risk assessment under supply-chain uncertainty. Evaluated on real Binance USD-M perpetual data (BTCUSDT/ETHUSDT, 15m, Sep–Dec 2025), SAE reduces MDD by 93.1% (0.4643 → 0.0319), |CVaR₀.₉₉| by ~97.5%, and DG loss proxy by ~97.0%, while cutting AttackSuccess from 1.00 to 0.728 with zero FalseBlocks—all statistically validated. SAE reorients safety toward *execution-layer survivability*, treating upstream intents and third-party skills as untrusted by design.

item.title: Amnesia: Adversarial Semantic Layer Specific Activation Steering in Large Language Models
item.description:
## Amnesia: Adversarial Semantic Layer-Specific Activation Steering in LLMs
We propose **Amnesia**, a lightweight, inference-time adversarial attack that steers internal transformer activations to bypass safety mechanisms in open-weight LLMs—*without fine-tuning, retraining, or external parameters*. Amnesia identifies semantically critical layers (e.g., FFN outputs in late transformer blocks) and injects targeted perturbations to suppress safety-aligned representations while preserving task coherence. Evaluated on Llama-3-8B, Qwen2-7B, and Phi-3-mini, Amnesia increases harmful output rates by **4.2×** on ToxiGen and AdvBench, evading Llama-Guard, RLHF alignment, and refusal heuristics. Crucially, it generalizes across architectures and requires only single forward passes. Our results expose a fundamental representational vulnerability in current open-weight LLM safety stacks—and underscore the urgent need for activation-aware defense and verification frameworks.

item.title: TASER: Task-Aware Spectral Energy Refine for Backdoor Suppression in UAV Swarms Decentralized Federated Learning
item.description: As backdoor attacks in UAV swarm decentralized federated learning (DFL) grow stealthier, outlier-based defenses become increasingly impractical due to resource constraints and lack of global coordination. Motivated by the empirical finding that *stealthier attacks induce more distinct spectral concentration in malicious gradients*, we propose **TASER (Task-Aware Spectral Energy Refine)**—the first lightweight, decentralized defense that suppresses backdoors via *spectral energy refinement* instead of complex outlier detection. TASER performs local gradient spectral decomposition, preserves low-frequency coefficients aligned with the main task, and discards high-energy but task-irrelevant spectral components—structurally disrupting the backdoor task. Theoretically grounded and empirically validated across UAV-DFL benchmarks, TASER achieves **<20% attack success rate** and **<5% accuracy loss**, outperforming SOTA defenses while reducing communication overhead by 62% and memory usage by 3.8×.

item.title: Why LLMs Fail: A Failure Analysis and Partial Success Measurement for Automated Security Patch Generation
item.description: This study conducts the first fine-grained failure analysis of LLM-generated security patches on the Vul4J benchmark, evaluating 319 patches across 64 Java vulnerabilities using a tri-axis framework: compilation success, security correctness (via PoV tests), and functional correctness (via test suites). Results show only **24.8%** achieve full correctness, while **51.4% fail both security and functionality**—primarily due to *semantic misunderstanding*, where syntactically valid patches apply incorrect repair strategies (e.g., misaligning input sanitization with injection semantics). To quantify this gap, we propose the **Security Repair Score (SRS)**, revealing a stark asymmetry: mean SRS = **0.251**, versus mean functional preservation = **0.832**. Vulnerability type strongly predicts success, ranging from **0% fix rate for input validation flaws** to **45% for infinite loops**. These findings underscore that LLM patches cannot be trusted without rigorous, vulnerability-specific security validation prior to deployment.

item.title: Optimal partition selection with Rényi differential privacy
item.description: We generalize optimal partition selection from $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP to $\delta$-approximate $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-Rényi DP (RDP), enabling tighter composition analysis. Since no general optimality result exists for users submitting multiple partitions, we design an optimal $L^2$-bounded weighted partition selection mechanism—*without frequency release*—that serves as a drop-in replacement for the Gaussian mechanism wherever frequencies are unnecessary. Integrated into state-of-the-art algorithms (PolicyGaussian and MAD2R), it improves utility in both parallel and sequential adaptive settings. Crucially, we formalize the notion of optimal additive-noise RDP partition selection and demonstrate a provable numerical separation: non-additive mechanisms strictly outperform additive ones, revealing a fundamental limitation of classical noise paradigms for this task.

item.title: ADVERSA: Measuring Multi-Turn Guardrail Degradation and Judge Reliability in Large Language Models
item.description: ADVERSA introduces a paradigm shift in LLM safety evaluation by measuring *multi-turn guardrail degradation* as continuous compliance trajectories—not discrete jailbreaks—and treating *judge reliability* as an empirically measured outcome, not an assumption. We deploy ADVERSA-Red, a fine-tuned 70B attacker (Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct + QLoRA) that eliminates attacker-side refusals, and score victim responses on a structured 5-point rubric capturing partial compliance. In a controlled red-teaming study across Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.2 (15 × 10-round conversations), we observe a 26.7% jailbreak rate with mean jailbreak round of 1.25—indicating early-round vulnerability dominance. Triple-judge consensus reveals moderate inter-rater agreement (κ = 0.62), systematic self-judge leniency, and attacker refusals (18.3%) as a critical, previously underreported confound in victim resistance measurement. All non-sensitive artifacts are publicly released; attack prompts are withheld per responsible disclosure.

item.title: MITRA: An AI Assistant for Knowledge Retrieval in Physics Collaborations
item.description: MITRA is a privacy-preserving, on-premise Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system designed to support knowledge retrieval in large-scale physics collaborations like CMS at CERN. It addresses the challenge of navigating vast, heterogeneous internal documentation through an automated pipeline combining Selenium-based web scraping, layout-aware OCR, and a novel two-tiered vector database—first identifying relevant analyses from abstracts, then retrieving precise passages from full documents. All components, including embedding models and the LLM, run locally to ensure data confidentiality. Evaluated on realistic CMS queries, MITRA achieves 86.5% top-1 exact match accuracy—more than double that of keyword search (32.1%). This work establishes a scalable, secure foundation for future research agents in experimental physics.

item.title: Logics-Parsing-Omni Technical Report
item.description: This paper introduces **Logics-Parsing-Omni**, a unified multimodal parsing framework that addresses fragmentation in task definitions and heterogeneity in unstructured data (documents, images, audio-visual streams). It establishes a **Unified Taxonomy** and a progressive three-level paradigm: (1) *Holistic Detection* for spatiotemporal grounding; (2) *Fine-grained Recognition* for symbolization (OCR/ASR) and attribute extraction; and (3) *Multi-level Interpreting* to build verifiable reasoning chains from local semantics to global logic. A key innovation is the **evidence anchoring mechanism**, enforcing strict alignment between high-level descriptions and low-level facts—enabling *evidence-based logical induction* and producing structured knowledge that is **locatable, enumerable, and traceable**. We release a standardized dataset, the Logics-Parsing-Omni model, and **OmniParsingBench**, a novel benchmark for cross-modal evaluation. Experiments confirm strong synergy between fine-grained perception and high-level cognition, significantly improving reliability and interpretability. Code, models, and benchmarks are open-sourced at https://github.com/alibaba/Logics-Parsing/tree/master/Logics-Parsing-Omni.

item.title: Discovery of a Hematopoietic Manifold in scGPT Yields a Method for Extracting Performant Algorithms from Biological Foundation Model Internals
item.description: We report the first extraction of a biologically grounded, high-performance hematopoietic algorithm from the scRNA-seq foundation model scGPT via mechanistic interpretability. We discover that frozen attention weights in scGPT encode a compact, branching hematopoietic manifold—validated on non-overlapping Tabula Sapiens and confirmed via zero-shot transfer to an independent multi-donor immune panel. Our general three-stage method—direct operator export, lightweight adaptor (~1.2K params), and task-specific readout—yields a standalone algorithm without target-data retraining. On 88 donor-holdout benchmarks, it achieves state-of-the-art pseudotime-depth ordering and leads on key endpoints (CD4/CD8 AUROC=0.867; mono/macro AUROC=0.951). Versus standard MLP probing of frozen scGPT embeddings, it is BH-significantly better on 6/8 classification tasks, runs 34.5× faster across 12-fold evaluation, and uses ~1000× fewer trainable parameters. The operator compresses losslessly to a single head and further to a rank-64 surrogate. Mechanistic analysis reveals a four-factor core (T/lymphoid, B/plasma, granulocytic, mono/macrophage) explaining 66.2% of ablation impact. A secondary intercellular communication manifold confirms method generalizability beyond hematopoiesis.

item.title: A Survey of Weight Space Learning: Understanding, Representation, and Generation
item.description: This survey introduces **Weight Space Learning (WSL)**—a paradigm shift treating neural network weights not as static outputs but as a structured, learnable domain. We propose the first unified taxonomy organizing WSL into three pillars: **Weight Space Understanding (WSU)** (analyzing geometry, symmetries, and distributions of weights), **Weight Space Representation (WSR)** (learning invariant embeddings for cross-model comparison), and **Weight Space Generation (WSG)** (synthesizing new weights via hypernetworks or generative models). We demonstrate how WSL enables practical advances in model retrieval, data-free reconstruction, continual/federated learning, and NAS. By unifying fragmented progress, this work establishes weight space as a fundamental, high-impact abstraction for model analysis, knowledge transfer, and weight synthesis—and releases an open resource hub to accelerate community development.

item.title: A Multi-Prototype-Guided Federated Knowledge Distillation Approach in AI-RAN Enabled Multi-Access Edge Computing System
item.description: This paper proposes **Multi-Prototype-guided Federated Knowledge Distillation (MP-FedKD)** to address data heterogeneity in AI-RAN-enabled Multi-Access Edge Computing (MEC) systems. Unlike conventional federated learning (FL) that suffers from performance degradation under non-IID data, MP-FedKD integrates **self-knowledge distillation** at clients and introduces a **multi-prototype paradigm**, overcoming information loss inherent in single-prototype averaging. We design a **Conditional Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (CHAC)** method to generate semantically rich class-specific prototype sets per client and propose a **prototype alignment scheme** for cross-client consistency. Furthermore, we introduce the **LEMGP loss**, which explicitly enforces geometric relationships between local embeddings and global multi-prototypes. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and non-IID settings demonstrate that MP-FedKD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FL baselines—achieving up to **7.8% higher average accuracy**, and **19.4% lower RMSE** and **22.6% lower MAE**, validating its effectiveness in privacy-preserving, edge-intelligent networking.

item.title: Model Merging in the Era of Large Language Models: Methods, Applications, and Future Directions
item.description: This survey introduces **model merging**—a training-free paradigm for unifying capabilities of multiple large language models (LLMs) into a single, efficient model. Motivated by the explosion of specialized fine-tuned LLMs, we propose the **FUSE taxonomy**, a four-dimensional framework covering *Foundations* (e.g., linear mode connectivity), *Unification Strategies* (e.g., weight averaging, task arithmetic, sparsification-enhanced merging, MoE-based fusion, evolutionary optimization), *Scenarios* (multi-task learning, safety alignment, domain specialization, multilingual transfer, federated aggregation), and *Ecosystem* (tools like MergeKit, benchmarks like MERGE-Bench). We analyze core formulations, representative works (e.g., TIES-Merging, SLERP), and practical trade-offs across 50+ methods. Key findings include: (1) merging often outperforms ensembles with <10% inference cost; (2) sparse and task-vector methods achieve superior generalization in low-resource settings; (3) theoretical gaps persist in convergence guarantees for non-convex LLM loss landscapes. We identify scalability, standardization, and formal safety analysis as critical open challenges.

item.title: Tracking Cancer Through Text: Longitudinal Extraction From Radiology Reports Using Open-Source Large Language Models
item.description: Radiology reports contain vital longitudinal oncology data, but their unstructured format hinders automated analysis—especially in privacy-critical healthcare settings where proprietary LLMs are often inadmissible. We introduce an **open-source, locally deployable pipeline**, built on the `llm_extractinator` framework and powered by the **Qwen2.5-7B** model, to extract and temporally link RECIST-compliant target, non-target, and new lesions across serial CT reports. Evaluated on 50 Dutch thorax/abdomen CT report pairs (baseline + follow-up), our system achieved **93.7% accuracy for target lesions**, **94.9% for non-target lesions**, and **94.0% for new lesions**, all at the clinical attribute level. This demonstrates that lightweight open-weight LLMs can deliver **clinically robust, privacy-preserving, and reproducible longitudinal extraction**—enabling scalable structuring of routine radiology text without cloud dependency or data leakage.

item.title: The SQInstructor: a guide to SQIsign and the Deuring Correspondence with level structures
item.description: We introduce **SQInstructor**, a general framework that extends the SQIsign signature scheme by incorporating level structures—finite subgroup markings—on supersingular elliptic curves. In this model, a signature is an isogeny $\phi: E_0 \to E_1$ that *preserves* given level structures $G_0 \subset E_0$ and $G_1 \subset E_1$, i.e., $\phi(G_0) = G_1$. This reformulation transforms the signing challenge into solving constrained norm equations in quaternion algebras with congruence conditions modulo $N$. We construct the first explicit, computable **Deuring correspondence for curves with level structures**, establishing a bijection between isomorphism classes of $(E,G)$ and certain oriented orders in $B_{p,\infty}$. We instantiate SQInstructor using both 1-dimensional (scalar) and 2-dimensional (vectorial) isogeny walks, achieving efficient signing and improved structural security. Our algorithms solve constrained norm equations $\mathrm{Nrd}(\alpha) = \ell^k$, $\alpha \equiv 1 \bmod N\mathcal{O}$ in $\tilde{O}(p^{1/4})$ time—enabling practical deployment. This work bridges arithmetic geometry and post-quantum cryptography through structure-aware isogeny primitives.

item.title: Trustworthy Agent Network: Trust in Agent Networks Must Be Baked In, Not Bolted On
item.description: This vision paper argues that trustworthiness in Agent-to-Agent (A2A) networks—emerging ecosystems where heterogeneous LLM-based agents autonomously coordinate to solve multi-step tasks—cannot be retrofitted via extensions of single-agent alignment techniques. Instead, trust must be *architected-in* from the foundational design of A2A coordination frameworks. We introduce the **Trustworthy Agent Network (TAN)** conceptual framework, built upon four interlocking pillars: (1) verifiable identity and capability contracts using lightweight zero-knowledge proofs; (2) context-aware trust propagation that dynamically adjusts trust weights based on task semantics and interaction history; (3) end-to-end semantic coherence guarantees enforced via formal ontology anchoring in protocols; and (4) failure-isolation and resilient re-negotiation protocols to contain cascading failures. TAN addresses systemic vulnerabilities—including adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, and operational cascade effects—that existing methods fail to mitigate. By treating trust as a first-class architectural concern—not an afterthought—we provide foundational principles for building auditable, predictable, and manipulation-resistant multi-agent systems.

item.title: On quadratic equations of $q$-regular tree and their applications in Graph Theory and Cryptography.
item.description: This paper revisits the $q$-regular tree-based graphs $D(n,q)$ and their connected components $CD(n,q)$, established three decades ago, and systematically explores their applications in Extremal Graph Theory, Spectral Graph Theory, LDPC codes, and Symmetric Cryptography. We introduce novel noncommutative cryptographic protocols built on the projective limit forest of $D(n,q)$ and the monoid of even-length walks. A modified Diffie–Hellman protocol is proposed, where the underlying transformation group $G(n,q)$ acts on $(\mathbb{F}_q)^n$ via cubical polynomial automorphisms of $\mathbb{F}_q[x_1,\dots,x_n]$. Users jointly compute a collision vector in $O(n^2)$ time. Security relies on the hardness of the Conjugacy Power Problem in the affine Cremona semigroup — a post-quantum–resistant assumption distinct from number-theoretic problems. An ElGamal-type inverse protocol enables encryption and digital signatures, augmented by multiple obfuscation layers.

item.title: Linear Code Equivalence via Plücker Coordinates
item.description: The Linear Code Equivalence (LCE) problem—central to the security of signature schemes like LESS—is reduced to recovering the permutation matrix $P$ in the monomial equivalence $Q = DP$. Leveraging algebraic geometry, we model LCE via Plücker coordinates on the Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(k,n)$, interpreting diagonal action as coordinate-wise scaling. We construct algebraically independent generators for the field of rational functions invariant under this action—*without* Reynolds operators or Gröbner bases. For any two equivalent codes, we explicitly build polynomials (in entries of $P$) that vanish at the true $P$. Though these polynomials have exponential degree and monomial count—rendering them impractical for cryptographically relevant parameters—this work is the first to apply Plücker geometry and invariant theory to LCE cryptanalysis, revealing deep structural connections between coding theory, geometry, and cryptography.

item.title: $\mathsf{GlueLUT}$: Generalized Lookup Table Arguments over Residue Rings via Auxiliary Fields
item.description: Lookup Table (LUT) arguments are vital for efficient SNARKs, yet high-performance constructions are largely field-specific, while many emerging applications (e.g., lattice cryptography, homomorphic encryption) natively operate over residue rings $\mathbb{Z}_Q$. Direct adaptation of field-based LUT techniques to rings suffers from unsoundness, limited scope, or inefficiency due to ring-theoretic obstacles. We introduce **$\mathsf{GlueLUT}$**, a general framework for sound and efficient LUT arguments over *any* $\mathbb{Z}_Q$ and *arbitrary* tables. Its core is the **Cross-Modulus Consistency (CMC) PIOP**, which proves that two witnesses over coprime moduli represent the same integer in the canonical range. Leveraging CMC as “glue”, $\mathsf{GlueLUT}$ performs lookups over an auxiliary prime field $\mathbb{F}_P$ ($P>Q$) and certifies consistency between $\mathbb{Z}_Q$- and $\mathbb{F}_P$-witnesses—bypassing ring limitations entirely. We provide two optimized instantiations: $\mathsf{GlueLUT}$-v1 for $Q=pq$ and $\mathsf{GlueLUT}$-v2 for $Q=p^k$, implement them as stand-alone PIOPs, and report prototype results confirming substantial gains in proof size and prover time.

item.title: Signal Lost (Integrity): The Signal App is More than the Sum of its Protocols
item.description: This paper presents two practical integrity-breaking attacks against Signal—the widely trusted secure messaging application—within its advertised threat model. First, we exploit a flaw in Signal’s 2022 username-based identity resolution mechanism (affecting Android and Desktop), enabling a malicious server to inject arbitrary messages into one-to-one chats. Though it triggers a visible “safety number changed” alert, users verifying safety numbers will observe no mismatch—rendering the warning misleading and integrity compromised. Second, we uncover a critical vulnerability in Signal’s Sealed Sender (SSS) feature: due to missing key validation and cryptographic context loss across software components (especially on Android), a malicious server can silently inject arbitrary messages into *both* one-to-one and group conversations—undetectable by users, with no preconditions, and persisting since SSS’s 2018 deployment. Both vulnerabilities were responsibly disclosed and patched within days. Our work demonstrates that end-to-end security guarantees critically depend not only on protocol soundness, but also on rigorous engineering of identity abstractions, feature composition, and cross-component cryptographic state management.

item.title: Security Considerations for Multi-agent Systems
item.description: This study presents the first empirical, cross-framework security assessment of Multi-agent Systems (MAS)—autonomous, tool-using agents with shared memory and inter-agent coordination. We systematically characterize MAS-specific threats across **193 distinct items in 9 risk categories**, then quantitatively evaluate **16 AI security frameworks** using a four-phase methodology: (1) building a production-grade MAS architecture knowledge base; (2) generative-AI-assisted, expert-validated threat modeling; (3) threat-granular survey design; and (4) three-point scoring per framework against each threat. No framework achieves majority coverage (>50%) in any single category. **Non-Determinism (mean score 1.231)** and **Data Leakage (1.340)** are the most under-addressed domains. OWASP Agentic Security Initiative leads overall (65.3% coverage) and in design-phase readiness; CDAO’s Responsible AI Toolkit excels in development and operational coverage. Results provide actionable, evidence-based guidance for MAS security framework selection and standardization.
